mnemonics.title: VIRTUal Function Rules
mnemonics.content:
V - Virtual keyword required
I - Inherited as virtual by default
R - Runtime polymorphism via vtable
T - This pointer passed implicitly
U - Using override for clarity
A - Abstract if pure (=0)
L - Late binding occurs

acronyms.expansion: Virtual TABLE - lookup table of function pointers for dynamic dispatch
acronyms.usage: Used by compiler to resolve virtual function calls at runtime

acronyms.expansion: Virtual PTR - hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable
acronyms.usage: Enables runtime determination of correct function implementation

acronyms.expansion: Pure Virtual Method - virtual function with no implementation (=0)
acronyms.usage: Defines interface without implementation in abstract classes

acronyms.expansion: Run-Time Type Identification - mechanism to identify object type at runtime
acronyms.usage: Used by dynamic_cast and typeid for type information

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box for each class (Base, Derived). Inside each box, create a table with function names as rows and function addresses as values. Show vptr arrow from object to its class vtable.
Create flowchart: Object → vptr → vtable → Function Address → Execute. Label each step showing runtime resolution.
Draw two scenarios: 1) Base obj = derivedObj (shows slicing - lost data), 2) Base* ptr = &derivedObj (shows polymorphism - retains behavior)

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant menu where the actual dish served depends on which branch (derived class) you visit, not just what's written on the menu (base class pointer).
Like a dispatch directory in an office - when a call comes for 'Manager', the directory points to whoever is currently the manager, not a fixed person.
Like a job posting template - it defines what the job requires but doesn't include a specific person filling that position yet.
Like a cleanup crew that starts from the top floor (derived) and works down to the lobby (base), ensuring nothing is missed.
